description.help: A short one line description of the checklist item.

requirement.help:
A complete requirement description of the checklist item
entered as a multi-line string.

dataformat.help:
Free text description of the type of data files acceptable as
checklist signoff validation.

rationale.help:
Rationale for the checklist item. Rationale should be a
unique alphanumeric code used by the standard or a short one line
or single word description.

criteria.help:
Simple list of signoff criteria for checklist item which
must all be met for signoff. Each signoff criteria consists of
a metric, a relational operator, and a value in the form
'metric op value'.

task.help:
Flowgraph job and task used to verify the checklist item.
The parameter should be left empty for manual and for tool
flows that bypass the SC infrastructure.

report.help:
Filepath to report(s) of specified type documenting the successful
validation of the checklist item.

default.help:
Filepath to report(s) documenting waivers for the checklist
item specified on a per metric basis.

ok.help:
Boolean check mark for the checklist item. A value of
True indicates a human has inspected the all item dictionary
parameters and verified they check out.

path.help:
Data directory path, this points the location where the data can be
retrieved or accessed.
Allowed roots:

* ``/path/on/network/drive``
* ``file:///path/on/network/drive``
* ``git+https://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``git://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``git+ssh://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``ssh://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``https://github.com/xyz/xyz/archive``
* ``https://zeroasic.com/xyz.tar.gz``
* ``github://siliconcompiler/lambdapdk/v1.0/asap7.tar.gz``
* ``python://siliconcompiler``

tag.help:
Data directory reference tag. The meaning of the this tag depends on the
context of the path.
For git, this can be a tag, branch, or commit id. For https this is the version
of the file that will be downloaded.

deps.help: List of named object dependencies included via add_dep().

default.help:
List of files grouped as a named set ('fileset'). The exact names of
filetypes and filesets must match the names used in tasks
called during flowgraph execution. The files are processed in
the order specified by the ordered file list.

topmodule.help: Name of top module specified on a per fileset basis.

idir.help:
Include paths specify directories to scan for header files during
compilation. If multiple paths are provided, they are searched
in the order given.

define.help:
Defines macros at compile time for design languages that support
preprocessing, such as Verilog, C, and C++. The macro format is
is `MACRONAME[=value]`, where [=value] is optional.

undefine.help:
Undefines a macro that may have been previously defined via the
compiler, options, or header files.

libdir.help:
Specifies directories to scan for libraries provided with the
:keypath:`Design,fileset,<fileset>,lib` parameter. If multiple paths are provided,
they are searched based on the order of the libdir list.

lib.help:
Specifies libraries to use during compilation. The compiler searches for
library in the compiler standard library paths and in the
paths specified by :keypath:`Design,fileset,<fileset>,libdir` parameter.

default.help:
Sets a named parameter to a string value. The value is limited to basic
data literals. The types of parameters and values supported is tightly
coupled to tools being used. For example, in Verilog only integer
literals (64'h4, 2'b0, 4) and strings are supported.

directly by the designer and tools. The example below shows how
all cells containing the string 'eco' could be marked as dont use
for the tool.

of the file that will be downloaded.

deps.help: List of named object dependencies included via add_dep().

default.help:
List of files grouped as a named set ('fileset'). The exact names of
filetypes and filesets must match the names used in tasks
called during flowgraph execution. The files are processed in
the order specified by the ordered file list.

topmodule.help: Name of top module specified on a per fileset basis.

idir.help:
Include paths specify directories to scan for header files during
compilation. If multiple paths are provided, they are searched
in the order given.

define.help:
Defines macros at compile time for design languages that support
preprocessing, such as Verilog, C, and C++. The macro format is
is `MACRONAME[=value]`, where [=value] is optional.

undefine.help:
Undefines a macro that may have been previously defined via the
compiler, options, or header files.

libdir.help:
Specifies directories to scan for libraries provided with the
:keypath:`Design,fileset,<fileset>,lib` parameter. If multiple paths are provided,
they are searched based on the order of the libdir list.

lib.help:
Specifies libraries to use during compilation. The compiler searches for
library in the compiler standard library paths and in the
paths specified by :keypath:`Design,fileset,<fileset>,libdir` parameter.

default.help:

data literals. The types of parameters and values supported is tightly
coupled to tools being used. For example, in Verilog only integer
literals (64'h4, 2'b0, 4) and strings are supported.

version.help:
Package version. Can be a branch, tag, commit hash,
or a semver compatible version.

vendor.help: Package vendor.

description.help:
Package short one line description for package
managers and summary reports.

license.help: Package list of SPDX license identifiers.

licensefile.help:
Package list of license files for to be
applied in cases when a SPDX identifier is not available.
(eg. proprietary licenses).

name.help:
Package author name provided with full name as key and
name as value.

email.help:
Package author email provided with full name as key and
email as value.

organization.help:
Package author organization provided with full name as key and
organization as value.

foundry.help:
Name of foundry corporation. Examples include intel, gf, tsmc,
samsung, skywater, virtual. The 'virtual' keyword is reserved for
simulated non-manufacturable processes.

node.help:
Approximate relative minimum dimension of the process target specified
in nanometers. The parameter is required for flows and tools that
leverage the value to drive technology dependent synthesis and APR
optimization. Node examples include 180, 130, 90, 65, 45, 32, 22 14,
10, 7, 5, 3.

stackup.help:
List of all metal stackups offered in the process node. Older process
nodes may only offer a single metal stackup, while advanced nodes
offer a large but finite list of metal stacks with varying combinations
of metal line pitches and thicknesses. Stackup naming is unique to a
foundry, but is generally a long string or code. For example, a 10
metal stackup with two 1x wide, four 2x wide, and 4x wide metals,
might be identified as 2MA4MB2MC, where MA, MB, and MC denote wiring
layers with different properties (thickness, width, space). Each
stackup will come with its own set of routing technology files and
parasitic models specified in the pdk_pexmodel and pdk_aprtech
parameters.

minlayer.help:
Minimum metal layer to be used for automated place and route
specified on a per stackup basis.

maxlayer.help:
Maximum metal layer to be used for automated place and route
specified on a per stackup basis.

wafersize.help:
Wafer diameter used in wafer based manufacturing process.
The standard diameter for leading edge manufacturing is 300mm. For
older process technologies and specialty fabs, smaller diameters
such as 200, 150, 125, and 100 are common. The value is used to
calculate dies per wafer and full factory chip costs.

unitcost.help:
Raw cost per unit shipped by the factory, not accounting for yield
loss.

d0.help:
Process defect density (d0) expressed as random defects per cm^2. The
value is used to calculate yield losses as a function of area, which in
turn affects the chip full factory costs. Two yield models are
supported: Poisson (default), and Murphy. The Poisson based yield is
calculated as dy = exp(-area * d0/100). The Murphy based yield is
calculated as dy = ((1-exp(-area * d0/100))/(area * d0/100))^2.

scribe.help:
Width of the horizontal and vertical scribe line used during die separation.
The process is generally completed using a mechanical saw, but can be
done through combinations of mechanical saws, lasers, wafer thinning,
and chemical etching in more advanced technologies. The value is used
to calculate effective dies per wafer and full factory cost.

edgemargin.help:
Keep-out distance/margin from the edge inwards. The edge
is prone to chipping and need special treatment that preclude
placement of designs in this area. The edge value is used to
calculate effective units per wafer/panel and full factory cost.

default.help:
List of filepaths to PDK device models for different simulation
purposes and for different tools. Examples of device model types
include spice, aging, electromigration, radiation. An example of a
'spice' tool is xyce. Device models are specified on a per metal stack
basis. Process nodes with a single device model across all stacks will
have a unique parameter record per metal stack pointing to the same
device model file. Device types and tools are dynamic entries
that depend on the tool setup and device technology. Pseudo-standardized
device types include spice, em (electromigration), and aging.

List of filepaths to PDK wire TCAD models used during automated
synthesis, APR, and signoff verification. Pexmodels are specified on
a per metal stack basis. Corner values depend on the process being
used, but typically include nomenclature such as min, max, nominal.
For exact names, refer to the DRM. Pexmodels are generally not
standardized and specified on a per tool basis. An example of pexmodel
type is 'fastcap'.

List of filepaths to PDK wire TCAD models used during automated
synthesis, APR, and signoff verification. Pexmodels are specified on
a per metal stack basis. Corner values depend on the process being
used, but typically include nomenclature such as min, max, nominal.
For exact names, refer to the DRM. Pexmodels are generally not
standardized and specified on a per tool basis. An example of pexmodel
type is 'fastcap'.

example: cli: -pdk_layermap 'asap7 klayout db gds M10 asap7.map'
default.help:
Files describing input/output mapping for streaming layout data from
one format to another. A foundry PDK will include an official layer
list for all user entered and generated layers supported in the GDS
accepted by the foundry for processing, but there is no standardized
layer definition format that can be read and written by all EDA tools.
To ensure mask layer matching, key/value type mapping files are needed
to convert EDA databases to/from GDS and to convert between different
types of EDA databases. Layer maps are specified on a per metal
stackup basis. The 'src' and 'dst' can be names of SC supported tools
or file formats (like 'gds').

example: cli: -pdk_layermap 'asap7 klayout db gds M10 asap7.map'
default.help:

one format to another. A foundry PDK will include an official layer
list for all user entered and generated layers supported in the GDS
accepted by the foundry for processing, but there is no standardized
layer definition format that can be read and written by all EDA tools.
To ensure mask layer matching, key/value type mapping files are needed
to convert EDA databases to/from GDS and to convert between different
types of EDA databases. Layer maps are specified on a per metal
stackup basis. The 'src' and 'dst' can be names of SC supported tools
or file formats (like 'gds').

example: cli: -pdk_layermap 'asap7 klayout db gds M10 asap7.map'

default.help:
Display configuration files describing colors and pattern schemes for
all layers in the PDK. The display configuration file is entered on a
stackup and tool basis.

klayout.help:
Display configuration files describing colors and pattern schemes for
all layers in the PDK. The display configuration file is entered on a
stackup and tool basis.

default.help:
Technology file containing setup information needed to enable DRC clean APR
for the specified stackup, libarch, and format. The 'libarch' specifies the
library architecture (e.g. library height). For example a PDK with support
for 9 and 12 track libraries might have 'libarchs' called 9t and 12t.
The standard filetype for specifying place and route design rules for a
process node is through a 'lef' format technology file. The
'filetype' used in the aprtech is used by the tool specific APR TCL scripts
to set up the technology parameters. Some tools may require additional
files beyond the tech.lef file. Examples of extra file types include
antenna, tracks, tapcell, viarules, and em.

path.help:
Data directory path, this points the location where the data can be
retrieved or accessed.
Allowed roots:

* ``/path/on/network/drive``
* ``file:///path/on/network/drive``
* ``git+https://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``git://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``git+ssh://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``ssh://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``https://github.com/xyz/xyz/archive``
* ``https://zeroasic.com/xyz.tar.gz``
* ``github://siliconcompiler/lambdapdk/v1.0/asap7.tar.gz``
* ``python://siliconcompiler``

tag.help:
Data directory reference tag. The meaning of the this tag depends on the
context of the path.
For git, this can be a tag, branch, or commit id. For https this is the version
of the file that will be downloaded.

deps.help: List of named object dependencies included via add_dep().

default.help:
List of files grouped as a named set ('fileset'). The exact names of
filetypes and filesets must match the names used in tasks
called during flowgraph execution. The files are processed in
the order specified by the ordered file list.

topmodule.help: Name of top module specified on a per fileset basis.

idir.help:
Include paths specify directories to scan for header files during
compilation. If multiple paths are provided, they are searched
in the order given.

define.help:
Defines macros at compile time for design languages that support
preprocessing, such as Verilog, C, and C++. The macro format is
is `MACRONAME[=value]`, where [=value] is optional.

undefine.help:
Undefines a macro that may have been previously defined via the
compiler, options, or header files.

libdir.help:
Specifies directories to scan for libraries provided with the
:keypath:`Design,fileset,<fileset>,lib` parameter. If multiple paths are provided,
they are searched based on the order of the libdir list.

lib.help:
Specifies libraries to use during compilation. The compiler searches for
library in the compiler standard library paths and in the
paths specified by :keypath:`Design,fileset,<fileset>,libdir` parameter.

default.help:
Sets a named parameter to a string value. The value is limited to basic
data literals. The types of parameters and values supported is tightly
coupled to tools being used. For example, in Verilog only integer
literals (64'h4, 2'b0, 4) and strings are supported.

depfileset.help: Sets the mapping for dependency filesets.

vendor.help: Package vendor.

description.help:

license.help: Package list of SPDX license identifiers.

licensefile.help:

site.help: Site names for a given library architecture.

input.help:
A list of inputs for this flowgraph node, where each input is
specified as a (step, index) tuple. This defines the dependencies
of this node.

default.help:
Weights specified on a per-node and per-metric basis, used by
optimization tasks (like 'minimum') to calculate a "goodness"
score for a run. The score is typically a weighted sum of
metric results.

Goals specified on a per-node and per-metric basis used to
determine whether a task run is considered successful. A task run
may be considered failing if the absolute value of any of its
reported metrics is larger than the goal for that metric (if set).
This is often used for metrics like 'errors' or 'setupwns'
where the goal is 0.

tool.help:
Name of the tool (e.g., 'openroad', 'yosys', 'builtin') that
this node will execute.

task.help:
Name of the task (e.g., 'place', 'syn', 'join') associated
with the node's tool.

taskmodule.help:
Full Python module path and class name of the task, formatted as
'<full.module.path>/<ClassName>'. This is used to import and
instantiate the correct Task class for setup and execution.

args.help:
User-specified arguments passed to the task's `setup()` method.
This allows for customizing a specific node's behavior without
affecting other nodes running the same task.

may be considered failing if the absolute value of any of its
reported metrics is larger than the goal for that metric (if set).
This is often used for metrics like 'errors' or 'setupwns'
where the goal is 0.

Name of the tool (e.g., 'openroad', 'yosys', 'builtin') that
this node will execute.

task.help:
Name of the task (e.g., 'place', 'syn', 'join') associated
with the node's tool.

taskmodule.help:
Full Python module path and class name of the task, formatted as
'<full.module.path>/<ClassName>'. This is used to import and
instantiate the correct Task class for setup and execution.

args.help:
User-specified arguments passed to the task's `setup()` method.
This allows for customizing a specific node's behavior without
affecting other nodes running the same task.

input.help:
A list of inputs for this flowgraph node, where each input is
specified as a (step, index) tuple. This defines the dependencies
of this node.

default.help:
Weights specified on a per-node and per-metric basis, used by
optimization tasks (like 'minimum') to calculate a "goodness"
score for a run. The score is typically a weighted sum of
metric results.

Goals specified on a per-node and per-metric basis used to
determine whether a task run is considered successful. A task run
may be considered failing if the absolute value of any of its
reported metrics is larger than the goal for that metric (if set).
This is often used for metrics like 'errors' or 'setupwns'
where the goal is 0.

errors.help:
Metric tracking the total number of errors on a
per step and index basis.

warnings.help:
Metric tracking the total number of warnings on a
per step and index basis.

memory.help:
Metric tracking total peak program memory footprint on a per
step and index basis.

exetime.help:
Metric tracking time spent by the EDA executable
:keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` on a
per step and index basis. It does not include the SiliconCompiler
runtime overhead or time waiting for I/O operations and
inter-processor communication to complete.

tasktime.help:
Metric tracking the total amount of time spent on a task from
beginning to end, including data transfers and pre/post
processing.

totaltime.help:
Metric tracking the total amount of time spent from the beginning
of the run up to and including the current step and index.

drvs.help:
Metric tracking the total number of design rule violations on a
per step and index basis.

drcs.help:
Metric tracking the total number of physical design rule violations on a
per step and index basis.

unconstrained.help:
Metric tracking the total number of unconstrained timing paths on a
per step and index basis.

cellarea.help: Metric tracking the total cell area (ignoring fillers) occupied by the design.

totalarea.help: Metric tracking the total physical die area occupied by the design.

macroarea.help: Metric tracking the total macro cell area occupied by the design.

padcellarea.help: Metric tracking the total io pad cell area occupied by the design.

stdcellarea.help: Metric tracking the total standard cell area occupied by the design.

utilization.help:
Metric tracking the area utilization of the design calculated as
100 * (cellarea/totalarea).

logicdepth.help:
Metric tracking the logic depth of the design. This is determined
by the number of logic gates between the start of the critital timing
path to the end of the path.

peakpower.help:
Metric tracking the worst case total peak power of the design specified on a per step
and index basis. Power metric depend heavily on the method
being used for extraction: dynamic vs static, workload
specification (vcd vs saif), power models, process/voltage/temperature.
The power peakpower metric tries to capture the data that would
usually be reflected inside a datasheet given the appropriate
footnote conditions.

averagepower.help:
Metric tracking the average workload power of the design specified on a per step
and index basis. Power metric depend heavily on the method
being used for extraction: dynamic vs static, workload
specification (vcd vs saif), power models, process/voltage/temperature.
The power averagepower metric tries to capture the data that would
usually be reflected inside a datasheet given the appropriate
footnote conditions.

leakagepower.help:
Metric tracking the leakage power with rails active but without any dynamic switching activity of the design specified on a per step
and index basis. Power metric depend heavily on the method
being used for extraction: dynamic vs static, workload
specification (vcd vs saif), power models, process/voltage/temperature.
The power leakagepower metric tries to capture the data that would
usually be reflected inside a datasheet given the appropriate
footnote conditions.

irdrop.help:
Metric tracking the peak IR drop in the design based on extracted
power and ground rail parasitics, library power models, and
switching activity. The switching activity calculated on a per
node basis is taken from one of three possible sources, in order
of priority: VCD file, SAIF file, 'activityfactor' parameter.

holdpaths.help:
Metric tracking the total number of timing paths violating hold
constraints.

setuppaths.help:
Metric tracking the total number of timing paths violating setup
constraints.

holdslack.help: Metric tracking the worst hold slack (positive or negative) on a per step and index basis.

holdwns.help: Metric tracking the worst negative hold slack (positive values truncated to zero) on a per step and index basis.

holdtns.help: Metric tracking the total negative hold slack (TNS) on a per step and index basis.

holdskew.help: Metric tracking the hold clock skew on a per step and index basis.

setupslack.help: Metric tracking the worst setup slack (positive or negative) on a per step and index basis.

setupwns.help: Metric tracking the worst negative setup slack (positive values truncated to zero) on a per step and index basis.

setuptns.help: Metric tracking the total negative setup slack (TNS) on a per step and index basis.

setupskew.help: Metric tracking the setup clock skew on a per step and index basis.

fmax.help: Metric tracking the maximum clock frequency on a per step and index basis.

macros.help:
Metric tracking the total number of macros in the design
on a per step and index basis.

cells.help:
Metric tracking the total number of cell instances in the design
on a per step and index basis.

registers.help:
Metric tracking the total number of register instances in the design
on a per step and index basis.

buffers.help:
Metric tracking the total number of buffer instances in the design
on a per step and index basis.

inverters.help:
Metric tracking the total number of inverter instances in the design
on a per step and index basis.

transistors.help:
Metric tracking the total number of transistors in the design
on a per step and index basis.

pins.help:
Metric tracking the total number of pins in the design
on a per step and index basis.

nets.help:
Metric tracking the total number of nets in the design
on a per step and index basis.

vias.help:
Metric tracking the total number of vias in the design
on a per step and index basis.

wirelength.help:
Metric tracking the total wirelength of the design on a per step
and index basis.

overflow.help:
Metric tracking the total number of overflow tracks for the routing
on per step and index basis. Any non-zero number suggests an over
congested design. To analyze where the congestion is occurring
inspect the router log files for detailed per metal overflow
reporting and open up the design to find routing hotspots.

userid.help: Record tracking the userid per step and index basis.

publickey.help: Record tracking the public key per step and index basis.

machine.help: Record tracking the machine name per step and index basis. (myhost, localhost, ...

macaddr.help: Record tracking the MAC address per step and index basis.

ipaddr.help: Record tracking the IP address per step and index basis.

platform.help: Record tracking the platform name per step and index basis. (linux, windows, freebsd)

distro.help: Record tracking the distro name per step and index basis. (ubuntu, redhat, centos)

arch.help: Record tracking the hardware architecture per step and index basis. (x86_64, rv64imafdc)

starttime.help: Record tracking the start time per step and index basis. Time is recorded with the format YYYY-MM-DD HR:MIN:SEC.MICROSEC for UTC

endtime.help: Record tracking the end time per step and index basis. Time is recorded with the format YYYY-MM-DD HR:MIN:SEC.MICROSEC for UTC

example: api: schema.set('record', 'region', '"US Gov Boston"', step='dfm', index=0)
region.help:
Record tracking the cloud region per step and index basis. Recommended naming methodology:

* local: node is the local machine
* onprem: node in on-premises IT infrastructure
* public: generic public cloud
* govcloud: generic US government cloud
* <region>: cloud and entity specific region string name

scversion.help: Record tracking the software version per step and index basis. Version number for the SiliconCompiler software.

toolversion.help:
Record tracking the tool version per step and index basis. The tool version captured corresponds to the 'tool'
parameter within the 'tool' dictionary.

toolpath.help:
Record tracking the tool path per step and index basis. Full path to tool executable used to run this
task.

example: api: schema.set('record', 'toolargs', '"-I include/ foo.v"', step='dfm', index=0)
toolargs.help: Record tracking the tool CLI arguments per step and index basis. Arguments passed to tool via CLI.

pythonversion.help: Record tracking the Python version per step and index basis. Version of python used to run this task.

osversion.help:
Record tracking the O/S version per step and index basis. Since there is not standard version system for operating
systems, extracting information from is platform dependent.
For Linux based operating systems, the 'osversion' is the
version of the distro.

kernelversion.help:
Record tracking the O/S kernel version per step and index basis. Used for platforms that support a distinction
between os kernels and os distributions.

toolexitcode.help: Record tracking the tool exit code per step and index basis.

remoteid.help: Record tracking the job ID for a remote run.

pythonpackage.help: Record tracking for the python packages installed.

status.help: Record tracking for the status of a node.

inputnode.help:
List of selected inputs for the current step/index specified as
(in_step, in_index) tuple.

path.help:
Data directory path, this points the location where the data can be
retrieved or accessed.
Allowed roots:

* ``/path/on/network/drive``
* ``file:///path/on/network/drive``
* ``git+https://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``git://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``git+ssh://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``ssh://github.com/xyz/xyz``

tag.help:
Data directory reference tag. The meaning of the this tag depends on the
context of the path.
For git, this can be a tag, branch, or commit id. For https this is the version
of the file that will be downloaded.

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

option.help:
List of command line options for the task executable, specified on
a per task and per step basis. Options must not include spaces.
For multiple argument options, each option is a separate list element.

default.help:
Environment variables to set for individual tasks. Keys and values
should be set in accordance with the task's documentation. Most
tasks do not require extra environment variables to function.

input.help:
List of data files to be copied from previous flowgraph steps 'output'
directory. The list of steps to copy files from is defined by the
list defined by the dictionary key :keypath:`flowgraph,<flow>,<step>,<index>,input`.
All files must be available for flow to continue. If a file
is missing, the program exists on an error.

output.help:
List of data files written to the 'output' directory of the
tool/task/step/index used in the keypath. All files must be available
for flow to continue. If a file is missing, the program exists on an error.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stdout.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDOUT is ignored.
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>.

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stdout
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stdout.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stderr.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDERR is ignored
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stderr
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stderr.

require.help:
List of keypaths to required task parameters. The list is used
by :meth:`.Project.check_manifest()` to verify that all parameters have been set up
before step execution begins.

default.help:
List of report files associated with a specific 'metric'. The file path
specified is relative to the run directory of the current task.

refdir.help:
Path to directories containing reference flow scripts, specified
on a per step and index basis.

script.help:
Path to the entry script called by the executable specified
on a per task and per step basis.

prescript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after reading in the design
but before the main execution stage of the step. Exact entry point
depends on the step and main script being executed. An example
of a prescript entry point would be immediately before global
placement.

postscript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after the main execution
stage of the step but before the design is saved.
Exact entry point depends on the step and main script being
executed. An example of a postscript entry point would be immediately
after global placement.

threads.help:
Thread parallelism to use for execution specified on a per task and per
step basis. If not specified, SC queries the operating system and sets
the threads based on the maximum thread count supported by the
hardware.

match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

warnings.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options

directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

option.help:
List of command line options for the task executable, specified on
a per task and per step basis. Options must not include spaces.
For multiple argument options, each option is a separate list element.

default.help:
Environment variables to set for individual tasks. Keys and values
should be set in accordance with the task's documentation. Most
tasks do not require extra environment variables to function.

input.help:
List of data files to be copied from previous flowgraph steps 'output'
directory. The list of steps to copy files from is defined by the
list defined by the dictionary key :keypath:`flowgraph,<flow>,<step>,<index>,input`.
All files must be available for flow to continue. If a file
is missing, the program exists on an error.

output.help:
List of data files written to the 'output' directory of the
tool/task/step/index used in the keypath. All files must be available
for flow to continue. If a file is missing, the program exists on an error.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stdout.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDOUT is ignored.
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>.

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stdout
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stdout.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stderr.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDERR is ignored
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stderr
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stderr.

require.help:
List of keypaths to required task parameters. The list is used
by :meth:`.Project.check_manifest()` to verify that all parameters have been set up
before step execution begins.

refdir.help:
Path to directories containing reference flow scripts, specified
on a per step and index basis.

script.help:
Path to the entry script called by the executable specified
on a per task and per step basis.

prescript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after reading in the design
but before the main execution stage of the step. Exact entry point
depends on the step and main script being executed. An example
of a prescript entry point would be immediately before global
placement.

postscript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after the main execution
stage of the step but before the design is saved.
Exact entry point depends on the step and main script being
executed. An example of a postscript entry point would be immediately
after global placement.

threads.help:
Thread parallelism to use for execution specified on a per task and per
step basis. If not specified, SC queries the operating system and sets
the threads based on the maximum thread count supported by the
hardware.

is missing, the program exists on an error.

output.help:

tool/task/step/index used in the keypath. All files must be available

destination.help:

none: the stream generated to STDOUT is ignored.
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,

output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>.

prescript.help:

but before the main execution stage of the step. Exact entry point
depends on the step and main script being executed. An example
of a prescript entry point would be immediately before global
placement.

postscript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after the main execution
stage of the step but before the design is saved.
Exact entry point depends on the step and main script being
executed. An example of a postscript entry point would be immediately
after global placement.

threads.help:

step basis. If not specified, SC queries the operating system and sets
the threads based on the maximum thread count supported by the
hardware.

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

warnings.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options

also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

input.help:

All files must be available for flow to continue. If a file
is missing, the program exists on an error.

output.help:

none: the stream generated to STDOUT is ignored.
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,

output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>.

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

warnings.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

warnings.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options

directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example

option.help:
List of command line options for the task executable, specified on
a per task and per step basis. Options must not include spaces.
For multiple argument options, each option is a separate list element.

default.help:
Environment variables to set for individual tasks. Keys and values
should be set in accordance with the task's documentation. Most
tasks do not require extra environment variables to function.

input.help:
List of data files to be copied from previous flowgraph steps 'output'
directory. The list of steps to copy files from is defined by the
list defined by the dictionary key :keypath:`flowgraph,<flow>,<step>,<index>,input`.
All files must be available for flow to continue. If a file
is missing, the program exists on an error.

output.help:
List of data files written to the 'output' directory of the
tool/task/step/index used in the keypath. All files must be available
for flow to continue. If a file is missing, the program exists on an error.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stdout.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDOUT is ignored.
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>.

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stdout
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stdout.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stderr.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDERR is ignored
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stderr
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stderr.

require.help:
List of keypaths to required task parameters. The list is used
by :meth:`.Project.check_manifest()` to verify that all parameters have been set up
before step execution begins.

refdir.help:
Path to directories containing reference flow scripts, specified
on a per step and index basis.

script.help:
Path to the entry script called by the executable specified
on a per task and per step basis.

prescript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after reading in the design
but before the main execution stage of the step. Exact entry point
depends on the step and main script being executed. An example
of a prescript entry point would be immediately before global
placement.

postscript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after the main execution
stage of the step but before the design is saved.
Exact entry point depends on the step and main script being
executed. An example of a postscript entry point would be immediately
after global placement.

threads.help:
Thread parallelism to use for execution specified on a per task and per
step basis. If not specified, SC queries the operating system and sets
the threads based on the maximum thread count supported by the
hardware.

is missing, the program exists on an error.

none: the stream generated to STDOUT is ignored.
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,

output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>.

retrieved or accessed.
Allowed roots:

* ``git://github.com/xyz/xyz``
* ``git+ssh://github.com/xyz/xyz``

* ``python://siliconcompiler``

context of the path.
For git, this can be a tag, branch, or commit id. For https this is the version
of the file that will be downloaded.

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

warnings.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

warnings.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options

option.help:
List of command line options for the task executable, specified on
a per task and per step basis. Options must not include spaces.
For multiple argument options, each option is a separate list element.

default.help:
Environment variables to set for individual tasks. Keys and values
should be set in accordance with the task's documentation. Most
tasks do not require extra environment variables to function.

input.help:
List of data files to be copied from previous flowgraph steps 'output'
directory. The list of steps to copy files from is defined by the
list defined by the dictionary key :keypath:`flowgraph,<flow>,<step>,<index>,input`.
All files must be available for flow to continue. If a file
is missing, the program exists on an error.

output.help:
List of data files written to the 'output' directory of the
tool/task/step/index used in the keypath. All files must be available
for flow to continue. If a file is missing, the program exists on an error.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stdout.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDOUT is ignored.
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>.

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stdout
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stdout.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stderr.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDERR is ignored
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stderr
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stderr.

require.help:
List of keypaths to required task parameters. The list is used
by :meth:`.Project.check_manifest()` to verify that all parameters have been set up
before step execution begins.

refdir.help:
Path to directories containing reference flow scripts, specified
on a per step and index basis.

script.help:
Path to the entry script called by the executable specified
on a per task and per step basis.

prescript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after reading in the design
but before the main execution stage of the step. Exact entry point
depends on the step and main script being executed. An example
of a prescript entry point would be immediately before global
placement.

postscript.help:
Path to a user supplied script to execute after the main execution
stage of the step but before the design is saved.
Exact entry point depends on the step and main script being
executed. An example of a postscript entry point would be immediately
after global placement.

threads.help:
Thread parallelism to use for execution specified on a per task and per
step basis. If not specified, SC queries the operating system and sets
the threads based on the maximum thread count supported by the
hardware.

context of the path.
For git, this can be a tag, branch, or commit id. For https this is the version
of the file that will be downloaded.

default.help:
Paths to software bill of material (SBOM) document file of the tool
specified on a per version basis. The SBOM includes critical
package information about the tool including the list of included
components, licenses, and copyright. The SBOM file is generally
provided as in a a standardized open data format such as SPDX.

path.help:
File system path to tool executable. The path is prepended to the
system PATH environment variable for batch and interactive runs. The
path parameter can be left blank if the :keypath:`tool,<tool>,task,<task>,exe` is
already in the environment search path.

vswitch.help:
Command line switch to use with executable used to print out
the version number. Common switches include ``-v``, ``-version``,
``--version``. Some tools may require extra flags to run in batch mode.

vendor.help:
Name of the tool vendor. Parameter can be used to set vendor
specific technology variables in the PDK and libraries. For
open source projects, the project name should be used in
place of vendor.

version.help:
List of acceptable versions of the tool executable to be used. Each
entry in this list must be a version specifier as described by Python
`PEP-440 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers>`_.
During task execution, the tool is called with the 'vswitch' to
check the runtime executable version. If the version of the system
executable is not allowed by any of the specifiers in 'version',
then the job is halted pre-execution. For backwards compatibility,
entries that do not conform to the standard will be interpreted as a
version with an '==' specifier. This check can be disabled by
setting :keypath:`option,novercheck` to True.

format.help: File format for tool manifest handoff.

default.help:
Defines a set of tool-specific environment variables used by the executable
that depend on license key servers to control access. For multiple servers,
separate servers with a colon. The named license variables are read at
runtime (:meth:`.Task.run()`) and the environment variables are set.

warningoff.help:
A list of tool warnings for which printing should be suppressed.
Generally this is done on a per design basis after review has
determined that warning can be safely ignored The code for turning
off warnings can be found in the specific task reference manual.

default.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

warnings.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example
illustrates the concept.

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

    $ grep WARNING place.log | grep -v "bbox" > place.warnings

SiliconCompiler::

    task.set('task', 'openroad', 'regex', 'place', '0', 'warnings',
             ["WARNING", "-v bbox"])

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,
the number of matches found for these regexes will be added to the
errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,
task, <task>, report` parameter for those metrics, if not already present.

errors.help:
A list of piped together grep commands. Each entry represents a set
of command line arguments for grep including the regex pattern to
match. Starting with the first list entry, each grep output is piped
into the following grep command in the list. Supported grep options
include ``-v`` and ``-e``. Patterns starting with "-" should be
directly preceded by the ``-e`` option. The following example

UNIX grep:

.. code-block:: bash

The "errors" and "warnings" suffixes are special cases. When set,

errors and warnings metrics for the task, respectively. This will
also cause the logfile to be added to the :keypath:`tool, <tool>,

option.help:
List of command line options for the task executable, specified on
a per task and per step basis. Options must not include spaces.
For multiple argument options, each option is a separate list element.

default.help:
Environment variables to set for individual tasks. Keys and values
should be set in accordance with the task's documentation. Most
tasks do not require extra environment variables to function.

input.help:
List of data files to be copied from previous flowgraph steps 'output'
directory. The list of steps to copy files from is defined by the
list defined by the dictionary key :keypath:`flowgraph,<flow>,<step>,<index>,input`.
All files must be available for flow to continue. If a file
is missing, the program exists on an error.

output.help:
List of data files written to the 'output' directory of the
tool/task/step/index used in the keypath. All files must be available
for flow to continue. If a file is missing, the program exists on an error.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stdout.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDOUT is ignored.
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>.

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stdout
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stdout.

destination.help:
Defines where to direct the output generated over stderr.
Supported options are:
none: the stream generated to STDERR is ignored
log: the generated stream is stored in <step>.<suffix>; if not in quiet mode,
it is additionally dumped to the display.
output: the generated stream is stored in outputs/<design>.<suffix>

suffix.shorthelp: Task: file suffix for redirected stderr
suffix.help: Specifies the file extension for the content redirected from stderr.
